title: To The Sighing Strephon.[1]
content:
1.

В В В В Your pardon, my friend,
В В В В If my rhymes did offend,
В В В В Your pardon, a thousand times o'er;
В В В В From friendship I strove,
В В В В Your pangs to remove,
В В В В But, I swear, I will do so no more.


2.

В В В В Since your beautiful maid,
В В В В Your flame has repaid,
В В В В No more I your folly regret;
В В В В She's now most divine,
В В В В And I bow at the shrine,
В В В В Of this quickly reformпїЅd coquette.


3.

В В В В Yet still, I must own, 
В В В В I should never have known,
В В В В From your verses, what else she deserv'd;
В В В В Your pain seem'd so great,
В В В В I pitied your fate,
В В В В As your fair was so dev'lish reserv'd.


4.

В В В В Since the balm-breathing kiss 
В В В В Of this magical Miss,
В В В В Can such wonderful transports produce; 
В В В В Since the "world you forget,
В В В В When your lips once have met,"
В В В В My counsel will get but abuse.


5.

В В В В You say, "When I rove,"
В В В В "I know nothing of love;"
В В В В Tis true, I am given to range;
В В В В If I rightly remember,
В В В В I've lov'd a good number; 
В В В В Yet there's pleasure, at least, in a change.


6.

В В В В I will not advance, 
В В В В By the rules of romance,
В В В В To humour a whimsical fair;
В В В В Though a smile may delight,
В В В В Yet a frown will affright,
В В В В Or drive me to dreadful despair.


7.

В В В В While my blood is thus warm,
В В В В I ne'er shall reform,
В В В В To mix in the Platonists' school;
В В В В Of this I am sure,
В В В В Was my Passion so pure,
В В В В Thy Mistress would think me a fool. 


8.

В В В В And if I should shun,
В В В В Every woman for one,
В В В В Whose image must fill my whole breast;
В В В В Whom I must prefer,
В В В В And sigh but for her,
В В В В What an insult 'twould be to the rest!


9.

В В В В Now Strephon, good-bye;
В В В В I cannot deny,
В В В В Your passion appears most absurd;
В В В В Such love as you plead,
В В В В Is pure love, indeed,
В В В В For it only consists in the word.
meaning:
"To The Sighing Strephon.[1]" is a quintessential example of George Gordon Byron's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
